title: Generator Runtime Guide
summary: Understand fuel capacity, consumption rate, load factor, reserve fuel, and practical backup runtime.

sections.title: What this tool does
body: This tool estimates generator runtime after load factor and reserve fuel are applied to the available fuel capacity.
bullets: Use it as a planning and sanity-check aid before the layout, rack, pathway, or support-room decision gets locked in.
Use it to document the assumptions behind a physical infrastructure decision.
Use it to catch design pressure before it becomes field trouble or rework.

sections.title: When to use it
body: Use it when planning backup power endurance, fuel storage, maintenance windows, outage scenarios, or recovery expectations for infrastructure support.
bullets: Early design planning.
Expansion review.
Existing-system cleanup or validation.
Explaining tradeoffs to a client, stakeholder, installer, or internal team.

sections.title: Input descriptions
body: These descriptions match the visible inputs on the Generator Runtime tool and explain how each value affects backup endurance.

items.label: Fuel Capacity (gal)
items.description: The usable amount of fuel available to the generator in gallons.
items.whyItMatters: Fuel capacity defines the starting endurance limit before reserve is held back.
items.commonMistake: Using tank size as if every gallon is practically usable.

items.label: Consumption Rate (gal/hr)
items.description: The fuel burn rate at the generator operating condition being modeled.
items.whyItMatters: Consumption rate determines how quickly the fuel supply is depleted.
items.commonMistake: Using a best-case fuel rate instead of the expected loaded condition.

items.label: Load Factor
items.description: The portion of rated load or operating demand applied to the generator.
items.whyItMatters: Generator fuel burn and endurance depend heavily on load.
items.commonMistake: Modeling runtime at light load when the real outage load is much higher.

items.label: Reserve Fuel (%)
items.description: The percentage of fuel intentionally held back instead of counted as usable runtime.
items.whyItMatters: Reserve protects against measurement error, refueling delays, and unexpected load changes.
items.commonMistake: Reporting maximum theoretical runtime with no fuel reserve.

sections.title: How to read the results
body: Healthy means the runtime appears to meet the entered endurance expectation with reserve. Watch means load or reserve assumptions should be reviewed. Risk means the generator may not support the desired outage duration.
bullets: Healthy: runtime has practical operating margin.
Watch: verify fuel curve, load, refueling plan, and reserve policy.
Risk: increase fuel capacity, reduce load, revise endurance expectations, or validate generator design.

sections.title: Common mistakes
body: Infrastructure planning problems usually come from treating the room, rack, pathway, power support, and service space as separate decisions. In the field, those limits stack together.
bullets: Sizing only the equipment and forgetting clearance, access, maintenance, and growth.
Using nominal dimensions without adding real cable, airflow, door swing, service, and replacement space.
Assuming a rack, floor, conduit, tray, UPS room, or generator can be pushed to the edge without operational side effects.
Failing to document assumptions before the layout becomes hard or expensive to change.

sections.title: Planning assumptions
body: This guide explains the planning logic behind the tool. It does not replace manufacturer installation documents, structural review, electrical design, local code requirements, or project-specific engineering judgment.
bullets: Manufacturer limits and installation instructions still matter.
Local code, AHJ direction, and site standards may change the final requirement.
Existing rooms, pathways, and support infrastructure can create limits that simple math does not show.
A design with reserve space and documented assumptions is usually easier to install, support, and expand.

sections.title: Professional responsibility
body: ScopedLabs tools are planning aids. They help surface assumptions and risk, but they do not replace formal engineering review, manufacturer validation, code compliance review, field measurement, or project-specific design responsibility.
bullets: Validate against the actual room, rack, pathway, equipment, and site conditions.
Confirm stakeholder expectations, service access, installation constraints, and operating requirements.
Document assumptions before implementation.
